architecture.summary: The paper uses sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to decompose LLM hidden states into interpretable features and identify causal sparse emotion features that drive emotion inference. Information flows from LLM activations through SAE decomposition to sparse feature analysis, then through causal intervention experiments (targeted steering of weak features and global optimization of steering vectors) to test whether identified features causally control emotion recognition.
trained_components: sparse autoencoders (SAEs) for decomposing LLM activations

biblio.title: Why Are Some Emotions Harder for LLMs? Uncovering the Causal Mechanisms of Emotion Inference via Sparse Autoencoders

claims.statement: Some emotions, such as surprise and fear, rely on highly concentrated feature sets, whereas disgust exhibits a more distributed sparse causal organization
Disgust causal features are generally weaker, frequently co-activate with features for other emotions, and are often overshadowed by causal features for anger
These representational differences provide a mechanistic explanation for why LLMs struggle more with certain emotions
Targeted steering of weaker causal features can mitigate emotion-specific failures
Global optimization of a steering vector over the identified causal features improves overall emotion recognition performance
